overview.goal: Repair the concrete v7.1 forward bottleneck: front-K capture must not scan all splats for every pixel.
overview.positioning: This notebook is explicit engineering reasoning, not hidden chain-of-thought.
overview.scope: Kernel shape, saved-state layout, binning strategy, and auxiliary-memory policy.

critique_of_previous_attempt.finding: The earlier v7.1 docs correctly attacked replay-backward, but they did not explicitly reject a capture implementation that rebuilt visibility with a full pixel-by-splat scan.
critique_of_previous_attempt.think: A saved-state backward is not enough if the saved state itself is produced by O(pixels times splats) work.
At 4K and 64k splats, a second pass with one thread per pixel and an all-splat loop recreates the same scaling wall even if the graphics render pass is fast.
Returning large front-K tensors to device memory in forward and then re-uploading them in backward adds bandwidth pain on top of the arithmetic pain.
critique_of_previous_attempt.conclusion: The branch needed two repairs, not one: sparse capture and less aux-state bouncing.

iteration.name: restate_the_actual_contract
iteration.problem: What must be true for hardware-forward plus front-K to help end-to-end training?
iteration.think: Common-path work has to scale with pixels times a small local visibility budget, not pixels times global splat count.
That means the candidate set for each pixel has to come from raster locality, binning, or an equivalent sparse visibility structure.
iteration.decision: Reject any design where front-K capture performs a full-scene scan for each pixel.

iteration.name: choose_a_sparse_candidate_source
iteration.problem: What is the simplest exact sparse source we can hand off in code?

options.option: Closest to pure graphics visibility, but materially more complex for a draft handoff.
Best long-term direction, but more machinery than this corrective pass needs.
Not the final ideal, but exact, explicit, and enough to kill the disastrous pixel-by-splat loop.

iteration.think: Because the prior handoff already copied projected inputs to CPU for packing, CPU-built tile bins are the most direct corrective step in a source handoff.
iteration.decision: Use depth-sorted CPU-built tile bins now, then let capture and overflow replay consume them on GPU.

iteration.name: preserve_exactness
iteration.problem: How do we make tile bins exact rather than heuristic?
iteration.think: A Gaussian is inserted into every tile touched by its alpha-threshold support box.
Inputs are already sorted by depth before packaging, so appending indices in that order preserves front-to-back ordering inside each tile list.
The kernel still runs the exact per-pixel visibility test before accepting a contributor, so bins only shrink the candidate set.

pseudocode.step: sort projected Gaussians by depth
for each Gaussian, compute the same threshold-support box used for its render quad
append the Gaussian id to every overlapped tile list
for each pixel, read only its tile list and run exact visibility tests in that order

iteration.decision: This preserves the exact common-path contributor sequence while changing the candidate enumeration cost.

iteration.name: repair_aux_state_bandwidth
iteration.problem: How do we stop forward from paying for unnecessary saved-state copies?
iteration.think: The earlier handoff cloned front-K buffers into tensors on the caller device, then backward copied them back to CPU-backed Metal buffers.
Keep the saved auxiliary tensors on CPU because they are only needed as autograd state, not as model-visible outputs.

pseudocode.step: read output texture into CPU memory
move only the visible image output to the caller device
keep packed Gaussians, out_cpu, front_ids, front_raw_alpha, front_meta, tile_offsets, and tile_ids on CPU for backward

iteration.decision: Saved-state movement becomes CPU-only aux storage instead of CPU to MPS to CPU round-trips.

iteration.name: trim_memory_without_math_drift
iteration.problem: Which saved tensors can be compressed exactly?
iteration.think: Per-batch Gaussian ids fit in uint16 for up to 65536 Gaussians.
front_count only needs a few bits when K is capped at 8, so count and overflow can share one uint8.
raw alpha stays float32 in the default path because it feeds exact backward math and is not the first place to trade accuracy.

decision.item: front_count plus overflow becomes packed uint8 front_meta

algorithm.intent: Create exact sparse candidate lists per screen tile.

pseudocode.step: tiles_x = ceil(width / tile_size)
tiles_y = ceil(height / tile_size)
for each batch and Gaussian in depth order:
compute alpha-threshold support bbox
convert bbox to tile ranges
increment per-tile counts
exclusive-scan counts into tile_offsets
second pass: append Gaussian ids into tile_ids using cursors

algorithm.think: This costs total tile references, not pixels times all splats.

algorithm.intent: Build exact front-K state from sparse tile-local candidates.

pseudocode.step: pixel determines its tile id
load start and end from tile_offsets
scan tile_ids[start:end] in front-to-back order
run exact pixel-local visibility test
save first K hits and raw alpha values
pack count plus overflow into front_meta

algorithm.expected_complexity: pixels times average tile bin size

algorithm.intent: Keep exact replay only for overflowing pixels, and only over local candidates.

pseudocode.step: if front_meta says no overflow, exit
determine the pixel's tile
scan only that tile's contributors in saved front-to-back order
replay exact compositing and accumulate gradients

algorithm.think: Even the expensive path stops pretending every overflow pixel must rediscover the whole scene.

tradeoffs.item: CPU-built bins are a corrective engineering step, not the final ideal architecture.
The remaining big future win is a GPU-resident tile bin builder or true raster-derived visibility capture.
Adversarial huge-support scenes can still generate very large tile-reference lists.

final_proposal.statement: Use v7.2 as the real fix for the concrete v7.1 bottleneck: sparse exact tile bins for capture and overflow replay, plus CPU-resident aux state and exact metadata packing.
Judge it next on Apple hardware. If the tile-binned shape holds but CPU packaging dominates, move the bin builder onto GPU in the next pass.
